flashcards.back: A technique ensuring that only one process can access a shared resource at a time in a distributed system where processes run on different machines connected via a network.

flashcards.front: What are the assumptions made in distributed mutual exclusion algorithms?
flashcards.back: Processes are cooperative, communication is reliable, messages are eventually delivered, and each process has a unique identifier.
A portion of code that accesses shared resources and must not be concurrently executed by more than one process.

flashcards.front: List the four properties that a distributed mutual exclusion algorithm must satisfy.
flashcards.back: Safety (mutual exclusion), Liveness (no starvation), Ordering (requests honored in timestamp order), and Fairness (no indefinite postponement).
A logical clock that assigns timestamps to events in a distributed system to establish partial ordering. It follows the rule: Li = Li + 1 on sending, Lj = max(Lj, Lj_msg) + 1 on receiving.
A token-based mutual exclusion algorithm using multicast and acknowledgments. A process requests entry to CS by sending REQUEST to all processes; enters only after receiving REPLY from all.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Ricart-Agrawala algorithm?
flashcards.back: 2(N-1) message exchanges: (N-1) for REQUEST messages and (N-1) for REPLY messages, where N is the number of processes.
A variation of Ricart-Agrawala that reduces message complexity by using quorum-based voting. Each process has a request set (quorum) of size √N.

flashcards.front: What is the message complexity of Maekawa's Algorithm?
flashcards.back: O(√N) messages per critical section entry, compared to O(N) in Ricart-Agrawala.

flashcards.front: What is the Token Ring Algorithm?
flashcards.back: Processes are arranged in a logical ring. A special token circulates around the ring; only the process holding the token can enter the critical section.

flashcards.front: What are the drawbacks of the Token Ring Algorithm?
flashcards.back: Token loss (must be regenerated), token duplication, and high latency (process may wait for token even if CS is free elsewhere).
A token-based algorithm that uses sequence numbers to detect stale requests. The token contains a queue of pending requests and sequence numbers.

flashcards.front: What is a Quorum in distributed systems?
flashcards.back: A subset of processes that must be contacted to obtain permission for critical section entry. Used in quorum-based algorithms like Maekawa.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Centralized and Distributed mutual exclusion?
flashcards.back: Centralized uses a single coordinator; distributed has no central coordinator, with responsibility shared among all processes.

flashcards.front: What is the Raymond's Tree-based Algorithm?
flashcards.back: A token-based mutual exclusion algorithm using a rooted tree structure where the token moves along tree edges, reducing message complexity to O(log N).
